cross_connections.display_label: Named for him — born same year Brainerd died, 1747

naming_inspiration.description: Named for the Connecticut missionary David Brainerd (X02351, 1718-1747), who died October 9, 1747 — just seven months after Brainard Hooker's birth. Brainerd was Connecticut-born (Haddam) and celebrated for his missionary work when the Hooker baby was named.

narrative_blocks.header: Revolutionary War soldier; led family north to Vermont
narrative_blocks.body: Brainard Hooker served in the Revolutionary War and moved his family from Wethersfield, Connecticut to Middlebury, Vermont, where he died in October 1808 at age sixty-one. He was survived by his wife Mary Deming Hooker, who outlived him by thirty-five years, dying in 1843 at age ninety.

narrative_blocks.header: Born in 1747 when David Brainerd also died
narrative_blocks.body: Brainard Hooker was born in Woodbury, Connecticut on March 4, 1747 — the same year that the renowned Presbyterian missionary David Brainerd died in Northampton, Massachusetts on October 9, 1747. The name 'Brainard' was rare in colonial Connecticut, but Brainerd was Connecticut-born (Haddam) and celebrated across New England for his missionary work among Delaware Indians; his parents almost certainly named the baby in honor of the dying missionary.

burial.plot_notes: Case Street Cemetery, Middlebury VT per FindAGrave

narrative_blocks.header: Wife Polly died in New York City
narrative_blocks.body: Calvin Hooker's wife Polly Kenyon Hooker died on March 10, 1835 in New York City — far from their home in Cayuga County — leaving Calvin a widower for twenty-four years until his own death in Victory, New York in 1859. Calvin outlived two siblings who also died in 1812: his sister Louisa, dead twenty-four days after her wedding, and his brother Harris, who died a prisoner of war at Quebec.

death.cause: Died prisoner of war, War of 1812

military_service.notes: Captured. Died prisoner of war at Quebec Canada.

narrative_blocks.header: War of 1812 prisoner; died at Quebec
narrative_blocks.body: Harris Hooker served as a soldier in the War of 1812 and was captured by British forces, dying as a prisoner of war in Quebec, Canada. His fate closely parallels that of Ezra Griswold Stiles — another Hooker descendant who was also captured in the War of 1812, documented in the story `ezra_griswold_stiles_war_of_1812_captivity`.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909), entries 483–2370

burial.plot_notes: Case Street Cemetery Middlebury VT per FA

narrative_blocks.header: Married September 12; died twenty-four days later
narrative_blocks.body: Louisa Hooker married Burnham Phelps on September 12, 1812, and died just twenty-four days later on October 6, 1812 — at twenty-one, in the same year her father Brainard died and her brother Harris died a prisoner of war at Quebec. Her husband married her sister Mary Deming Hooker fourteen months later, who gave him the family the brief first marriage could not.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909), entries 483–2370

narrative_blocks.header: Married her late sister's husband; bore explorer-named twins
narrative_blocks.body: Fourteen months after her sister Louisa's death — just twenty-four days into Louisa's marriage to Burnham Phelps — Mary Deming Hooker married Burnham herself on January 7, 1814, the practice of marrying a deceased wife's sister recognized in New England as a form of family continuity. Among their eight children were twins born July 7, 1823 named Christopher Columbus and Americus Vespusius — honoring both Atlantic explorers in a single act of naming, though Americus died at age three in 1826.

narrative_blocks.header: Died at thirty-nine
narrative_blocks.body: Jesse Hooker died in 1791 at approximately age thirty-nine, leaving his wife Sabrina Smith Hooker a widow with six children, the youngest aged five. Sabrina went on to marry John Holmes after Jesse's death.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)

narrative_blocks.header: Wife died in September
narrative_blocks.body: Hezekiah Hooker's wife Lovice Roe Hooker died on September 20, 1852, and Hezekiah died less than four months later, on January 13, 1853, in Danby, New York. They had raised twelve children together, all born in Dryden, New York.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)

narrative_blocks.header: Twin sister of Hezekiah; lived to sixty-four
narrative_blocks.body: Lucy Hooker was born as a twin with her brother Hezekiah on May 19, 1778 in Sheffield, Massachusetts. She died in 1842 at approximately age sixty-four.

burial.plot_notes: Maplewood Cemetery Henrietta Monroe County NY per FA

narrative_blocks.header: Outlived two husbands; died at Henrietta at eighty-seven
narrative_blocks.body: Abigail Hooker Pixley buried her first husband Isaac Pixley in 1790 when she was thirty-six — left with six children, the youngest barely two — remarried James Root in 1793, bore another child, then buried Root in 1802 when she was forty-eight, and spent the next thirty-nine years a widow in Henrietta, New York, dying on January 6, 1841 at age eighty-seven. She had migrated from Woodbury, Connecticut to the Berkshire hills of Massachusetts as a young wife, and then west again to upstate New York as an old widow, the arc of her life tracking the westward movement of her entire generation — and she outlived every chapter of it, the Puritan inheritance carried through two husbands, seven children, and eighty-seven years.

burial.plot_notes: Maplewood Cemetery Henrietta Monroe County NY per FA — NOTE: Isaac died at Great Barrington MA in 1790; burial at Henrietta NY CEM497 may be erroneous FA data entry or remains were reinterred by Abigail later

narrative_blocks.header: Innkeeper of Great Barrington
narrative_blocks.body: Isaac Pixley kept a tavern at Great Barrington, Massachusetts, that burned in 1787 — the Pixley genealogy notes the fire 'is said to have been incendiary,' suggesting arson — and after losing the building he built the old red house that David Humphrey would later occupy for many years; his wife's sister Anna Hooker, present at the fire, fractured her leg badly jumping from the second-story window to frozen ground. He died three years later, on May 11, 1790, at Great Barrington, age forty — leaving Abigail with six children under seventeen — and she would outlive him by fifty-one years, eventually dying in Henrietta, New York in 1841 at eighty-seven.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)

narrative_blocks.header: His great-grandson won the Nobel Peace Prize
narrative_blocks.body: James Root's first wife was Lydia Anna Robb (1749-1788 of Westfield, Massachusetts), and their son Elihu Root Sr. (born 1772) began a family line that produced Oren Root (born 1803) and then Elihu Root (born 1845) — Secretary of War under President McKinley, Secretary of State under Theodore Roosevelt, and winner of the Nobel Peace Prize in 1912 for his work in international arbitration. When James Root then married Abigail Hooker as his second wife in 1793, he connected his family to the descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker — meaning the Pixley-Hooker family and the Elihu Root line share a common progenitor in James Root, though the Nobel laureate descended from Lydia Robb's branch and carries no Hooker blood.

burial.plot_notes: East Bloomfield Cemetery Ontario County NY per FA. Epitaph: 'Age 80 Years. One of the early settlers of East Bloomfield, Ontario County, New York'

narrative_blocks.header: One of the early settlers of East Bloomfield
narrative_blocks.body: Ira Pixley was born at Great Barrington, Massachusetts in 1783 — in the same Berkshire hills where his parents' tavern burned in 1787 and his father died in 1790 — and by 1809 had moved his family west to East Bloomfield, Ontario County, New York, where he and Sarah Adams married that August and where he would spend the rest of his life. His gravestone at East Bloomfield Cemetery calls him simply 'one of the early settlers of East Bloomfield, Ontario County, New York' — the son of an innkeeper who died young, become himself a founding farmer of an upstate New York township, eighty years old when he died there in 1863.

narrative_blocks.header: She died April 1866
narrative_blocks.body: Fanny Pixley married James Sperry in Great Barrington in January 1812 and they lived at Henrietta, New York, raising eight children across three decades; she died April 29, 1866 in Rochester at approximately seventy-eight, and James outlived her by almost exactly eleven months — dying March 29, 1867 also in Rochester. The Pixley genealogy corrects the EH date: Fanny died in 1866, not 1867 — she went first, after fifty-four years of marriage, and James followed in the same city the following spring.

military_service.notes: EH: 'He served in the Quarter-master's Department in the Revolutionary War.'

narrative_blocks.header: Quartermaster's Department veteran; lived ninety years
narrative_blocks.body: Gilbert Hooker served in the Quartermaster's Department during the Revolutionary War, then settled at Stillwater, New York with his wife Rebecca Andrews where their children were born, later moving the family to Clyde, New York — and lived to see all of it: the nation he helped supply through the Revolution, the expansion across New York State, the rise of his son James Harvey to wealthy merchant at Troy. He died January 9, 1849 at approximately ninety years of age, having outlived his wife Rebecca by eight years — born in Woodbury, Connecticut in 1758, dying when Abraham Lincoln was still an Illinois congressman and the gold rush was just beginning in California, his life spanning the entire arc from Puritan New England colony to young republic.

burial.plot_notes: Maple Grove Cemetery Clyde Wayne County NY per FA

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)

bio.photo_notes: Portrait of Colonel James H. Hooker by Nelson Cook, ca. 1845-1848. Private Collection, California. Restored 2026.

burial.plot_notes: Oakwood Cemetery Troy NY per private collection research

notable.notable_blurb: Erie Canal and Hudson River transportation tycoon

career.organization: Troy Tow-Boat Line; Troy and Whitehall Towing Company; Northern Transportation Line; Pattison & Hart
career.notes: Started as business clerk at Pattison & Hart after moving to Troy ~1820. Built extensive freight operation on Erie, Oswego, and Champlain Canals, Lake Champlain, and Hudson River. By 1851: 11 barges (5,100 tons), 120 canal boats to Buffalo/Rochester/Oswego/Whitehall, 11 sail vessels and steam propeller 'James H. Hooker' on Lake Champlain, 6 steamboats + 5 propellers on Buffalo Canal Line, 800 horses on Erie Canal, 50 agents throughout NY state. Never married.

narrative_blocks.header: A steamship on Lake Champlain bore his name
narrative_blocks.body: By 1850, Colonel James H. Hooker operated one of the most extensive freight transportation networks in the American northeast — 50 canal boats to Buffalo, 29 to Rochester, 16 to Oswego, 25 to Whitehall, eleven barges totaling 5,100 tons, six steamboats and five propellers on the Buffalo Canal Line, eleven sail vessels on Lake Champlain, and a steam propeller named the 'James H. Hooker' carrying 300 tons between Albany and Montreal.

narrative_blocks.header: Violent in his speech; fair with his workers
narrative_blocks.body: James Harvey Hooker never married — his extended family knew him as 'Uncle James' while 20th century descendants called him 'the Fat Man' for his portly girth — and contemporaries described him as 'a passionate eccentric, violent in his speech and action, but ultimately fair and just with his workers and associates.' His portrait, painted by Nelson Cook in the period 1845-1848 when Cook was at his mature peak, survived in a California private collection and was cleaned and restored in February 2026 by Susan Brown-Madorsky of SB Conservation in Laguna Beach — 175 years after its subject died in Troy on December 28, 1851.

bio.bio_blurb: Hooker descendant who tracked a 250-year-old family clock

burial.plot_notes: West Clarendon Cemetery Clarendon Calhoun County Michigan per FA

narrative_blocks.header: Thomas Hooker’s clock was still keeping time
narrative_blocks.body: A clock the Hartford founder carried from England in the 1630s was still ticking in a Grand Rapids parlor when her obituary ran in 1885 — two and a half centuries, an ocean, and a continent later, passed sideways through cousins rather than down a single line.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)

narrative_blocks.header: Died at thirty; infant Joseph eight months old
narrative_blocks.body: Ann Hooker Congdon married Joseph Congdon on October 2, 1823 and bore six children in eleven years; her youngest, Joseph, was born April 17, 1834, and she died December 17 that same year at Freedom, New York, at thirty years old — leaving an eight-month-old infant among her surviving children. Her husband Joseph Congdon outlived her by thirty-eight years, dying at Elmira in 1872; the eldest child she had named 'James Hooker' had himself died nine months before that, in May 1865, leaving her family's history as a study in how early death cascades through a generation.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)

narrative_blocks.header: Which man she married remains uncertain
narrative_blocks.body: EH identified Lucy Hooker's husband as 'Mr. Aldrich,' but FA and the obituary of her sister Elizabeth decisively show Elizabeth married Deach Aldrich — meaning EH appears to have swapped the two husbands, and the correct identity of Lucy's husband is now unknown. She died July 31, 1850 at forty-two, five years after her father Gilbert Hooker died at ninety and outliving him barely — both buried, one presumes, in the upstate New York and Indiana counties where the family had scattered across three generations.

migration.notes: Among the first settlers of Bethlehem Parish, Woodbury. One of the leading men of the town for many years.

narrative_blocks.header: Among the first settlers of Bethlehem Parish
narrative_blocks.body: Around 1720 he was among the first settlers of Bethlehem Parish in Woodbury, Connecticut. He was described as 'for many years one of the leading men of the town.'

narrative_blocks.header: Died suddenly in 1756
narrative_blocks.body: Edward Hooker's record says he 'died suddenly' in February 1756, at age sixty-seven. The unexpected death cut short his role as one of the leading men of Bethlehem Parish — a settlement he had helped establish thirty-five years before.

bio.notes: Also spelled Curtiss in some records

sources.full: L.J. Hooker, 'Edward Hooker, Commander, U.S. Navy,' The Old Northwest Genealogical Quarterly (Columbus, Ohio, 1898-), VII, 12

cross_connections.notes: Col. Wadsworth's son Daniel Wadsworth founded the Wadsworth Atheneum in Hartford — a monument with Hooker family kinship interest. Research needed to confirm Mehitable Russell's Hooker descent line. Warren 1887 confirms: Elizabeth Standley married John Wadsworth — their son Daniel Wadsworth founded the Wadsworth Atheneum.
cross_connections.display_label: was his father-in-law, honored in the Indian wars

cross_connections.notes: Capt. Thomas Hart b. ca. 1644, d. Aug 27, 1726 age 82. Son of Stephen Hart (X00034). Brother of John Hart who survived the 1666 fire (X00033). Not in this tree through marriage — noted as civic context only.

burial.plot_notes: Memento Mori Cemetery, Farmington, Hartford County, Connecticut. Same cemetery as his father Rev. Samuel Hooker (H00007).

notable.primary_url_label: Native Northeast Portal — John Hooker biography

narrative_blocks.header: Judge of Connecticut Colony's Supreme Court
narrative_blocks.body: He served as Judge of the Supreme Court of Connecticut Colony from 1724 to 1732 and as Speaker of the Lower House. He also served as witness, certifying Assistant, and Registrar in the 1733 Sue Squa land deed — three official roles in a single transaction.

narrative_blocks.header: He defended Tunxis land rights in court
narrative_blocks.body: In 1738, John Hooker provided a formal statement to the Connecticut General Court that ninety acres at Indian Neck had 'always belonged' to the Tunxis tribe — an unusual act of testimony on behalf of the indigenous people whose land his own ancestors had settled near.

narrative_blocks.header: The elm fell so someone could walk straight
narrative_blocks.body: His father gave him a site on the west side of Farmington's main street, where he built under the branches of a great elm. The tree became famous through all the region for the company that gathered beneath it — three generations of Hookers lived in its shade before a later owner cut it down to straighten the sidewalk.

narrative_blocks.header: The bell tolled — Hooker stepped out first
narrative_blocks.body: A chronicler recorded that every Sunday the bell rang, then began tolling; at the first stroke, Hooker issued from his house and proceeded to the Meeting House. The minister walked from his house. Whoever arrived first waited at the door; together they entered — at which the bell stopped and all the congregation arose.

narrative_blocks.header: The Sabbath ended when Hooker touched his gate
narrative_blocks.body: The holiness of Sunday was supposed to end at sunset — but no child in the town dared stir until John Hooker gave the signal. When the sun sank, he walked to his gate and leaned on it; open flew the doors and out came the children with a wild rush and shout.

narrative_blocks.header: Dark hair, dark eyes — dignity commanding respect
narrative_blocks.body: His grandson described him: "Dark hair and dark eyes, and a dignity in his air and expression that procured profound respect." A fine looking man, tall, very erect, and had a prominent large nose. He lived his entire life in Farmington, dying there in 1746 at age 81.

career.notes: Served as justice and register of Farmington
From Farmington. Served as Clerk two sessions; Speaker six sessions.
Served as judge of Superior Court concurrent with assistant role
Commissioner of the Mohegan's sequestered land at New London

cross_connections.display_label: Both married Hooker brothers — from two separate Stanley families.
Their families united — three daughters married Hart brothers.
Her sister Elizabeth Standley married into the Wadsworth line.

narrative_blocks.header: Her father won distinction in the Indian wars
narrative_blocks.body: Capt. John Standley was a man of wealth and high social position who had served as lieutenant and captain in the Indian wars. Her sister Elizabeth Standley's granddaughter married Col. Jeremiah Wadsworth, Commissary General of the Continental Army — Wadsworth's wife also descended from Rev. Thomas Hooker, making Wadsworth's children doubly connected to the Hooker line.

bio.notes: Also spelled 'Stanley' in EH entry 30; 'Standley' in entries 99-101

burial.plot_notes: Memento Mori Cemetery, Farmington CT. SOURCE: FindAGrave.
